critic_raw:
This multimodal neural network for post-operative complication prediction design has a label construction problem, an evaluation metric selection issue, and a text embedding design risk.

The outcome is any of six distinct complications (surgical site infection, VTE, pneumonia, AKI, readmission, return to OR) within 90 days. These complications have very different clinical mechanisms, prevalence rates, and predictors: SSI is a local wound process predicted by surgical technique and sterility; VTE is a systemic coagulation process predicted by immobility and prophylaxis; AKI is a renal process predicted by contrast exposure and baseline creatinine. Aggregating these six distinct outcomes into a single composite binary label creates a label that is mechanistically heterogeneous. A model that learns any one of these pathways will receive positive signal on the composite label, but may fail to detect other complication types. The design does not report per-complication performance, so there is no way to determine whether the multimodal model improves on all complication types or only some.

The primary metric is 'sensitivity at 85% fixed specificity.' This metric is selected on validation data and then the same threshold is applied at test time. However, the validation and test sets are different temporal windows with potentially different complication rates and case mix (the design stratifies by surgeon and complication rate quartile, but does not guarantee identical overall complication rates). If the complication rate shifts between validation and test periods, the threshold optimized for 85% specificity on validation will not achieve 85% specificity on the test set, making the primary metric non-reproducible.

The text embeddings use a pretrained clinical LM (SciBERT or BioBERT fine-tuned on MIMIC), with frozen pretrained embeddings passed through pooling and FC layers. Operative reports contain highly specific procedural terminology (implant names, anatomical landmarks, intraoperative findings) that MIMIC-based pretraining may not cover adequately for orthopedic surgery. Using frozen embeddings means the model cannot adapt the representation to orthopedic-specific language, and the FC layers on top of the frozen embeddings must compress a high-dimensional generic embedding into a task-relevant surgical complications representation — a substantial information bottleneck.

The design uses 'all patients undergoing orthopedic surgery with complete structured data' as the cohort, selecting for completeness. Complete-data patients in a surgical EHR system may be systematically different from incomplete-data patients (e.g., more complex cases generate more documentation), introducing selection bias that affects the estimated complication rate and the generalizability of the model.
all_issues_raised: The composite binary label aggregates six mechanistically distinct complications (SSI, VTE, pneumonia, AKI, readmission, return to OR) with different predictors and base rates; a model that detects one complication pathway will receive positive gradient on the composite label while failing others, and without per-complication performance reporting the design cannot determine where improvement actually occurs.

The primary metric (sensitivity at 85% fixed specificity) is threshold-dependent; if complication rate shifts between the validation and test temporal windows, the threshold optimized on validation will not reproduce 85% specificity at test time, making the primary metric structurally inconsistent across splits.

Frozen MIMIC-based clinical LM embeddings are unlikely to adequately represent orthopedic-surgery-specific procedural terminology (implant names, intraoperative findings); FC layers on top of frozen generic embeddings cannot recover orthopedic-specific representations, limiting what the text modality can contribute.

Selecting only complete-data patients systematically excludes certain case types (incomplete documentation is often associated with clinical complexity or rapid deterioration), introducing selection bias that inflates complication rates or distorts predictor-outcome relationships in ways that do not reflect the full surgical population.
all_issues_adjudicated: The composite binary label aggregates six mechanistically distinct complications (SSI, VTE, pneumonia, AKI, readmission, return to OR) with different predictors and base rates; a model that detects one complication pathway will receive positive gradient on the composite label while failing others, and without per-complication performance reporting the design cannot determine where improvement actually occurs.

The primary metric (sensitivity at 85% fixed specificity) is threshold-dependent; if complication rate shifts between the validation and test temporal windows, the threshold optimized on validation will not reproduce 85% specificity at test time, making the primary metric structurally inconsistent across splits.

Frozen MIMIC-based clinical LM embeddings are unlikely to adequately represent orthopedic-surgery-specific procedural terminology (implant names, intraoperative findings); FC layers on top of frozen generic embeddings cannot recover orthopedic-specific representations, limiting what the text modality can contribute.

Selecting only complete-data patients systematically excludes certain case types (incomplete documentation is often associated with clinical complexity or rapid deterioration), introducing selection bias that inflates complication rates or distorts predictor-outcome relationships in ways that do not reflect the full surgical population.
